sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision and career trajectory (g.1.a) from student awards to leading independent projects. It presents distinctive ideas (g.1.b) regarding health literacy in care workers and AI for discharge summaries. The training plan (g.1.c) is comprehensive, covering systematic reviews, qualitative methods, and AI. Mentorship (g.1.d) is robust, with four named supervisors and a structured meeting schedule.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details identified for this section.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details identified for this section.

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly articulates why this award is the right next step (g.2.a), moving from a fellowship to a PhD to build independence. The difference the award makes (g.2.b) is clear: enabling a career in academia and bridging health/social care. It builds on existing experience (g.2.c) by adding qualitative and AI skills. Partnerships (g.2.d) are well-developed with Vivaldi, NHS trusts, and the Doubleday Centre.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details identified for this section.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details identified for this section.

sub_criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant has extensive relevant experience (g.3.a), including leading a cross-sectional survey and analyzing data in R. Formal training (g.3.b) includes an MPH with Distinction and MFPH. There is strong alignment (g.3.c) between prior work in health literacy and the proposed project's focus on discharge summaries and care worker literacy.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details identified for this section.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details identified for this section.

sub_criteria.name: Impactful Research Outputs and Outcomes
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful research outputs or outcomes appropriate to the applicant’s career stage, demonstrating contribution to the field.

signals.signal_text: Number and quality of publications, reports, or presentations
Citation metrics or other indicators of impact (where mentioned)
Awards, grants, or recognitions linked to prior research
Description of practical or societal impact of previous work

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant lists multiple high-quality publications (g.4.a) in journals like Anaesthesia and British Journal of Surgery. Awards and recognitions (g.4.c) include the Doubleday Student Award and Best Poster Presentation. Practical impact (g.4.d) is evidenced by study findings leading to improved appointment letters and mandatory training. Citation metrics (g.4.b) are not explicitly detailed, though the quality of journals implies impact.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While publication quality is high, specific citation counts or impact factors are not explicitly listed in the text, preventing a perfect score for g.4.b.
sub_criteria.rationale: While publication quality is high, specific citation counts or impact factors are not explicitly listed in the text, preventing a perfect score for g.4.b.

sub_criteria.name: Trajectory Towards Research Leadership
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates potential and progression towards becoming a future leader in health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Statements of career ambitions and long-term goals
Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant clearly states career ambitions (g.5.a) to become a public health academic. Evidence of increasing responsibility (g.5.b) includes leading a team of 7, organizing conferences, and serving as a judge. Participation in leadership programs (g.5.c) includes facilitator training and executive coaching. Recognition (g.5.d) includes awards and being appointed as a visiting lecturer.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details identified for this section.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details identified for this section.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Practitioner-Academic Career (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: For applicants including a clinical or practice element, shows a clear commitment to combining research with professional practice.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of practitioner-academic career intentions
Involvement in clinical or practice-based research
Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities
Participation in relevant professional networks or qualifications

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states intentions (g.6.a) to pursue a career at the interface of health and social care. Involvement in practice-based research (g.6.b) is demonstrated by conducting studies while training as a junior doctor. Balancing responsibilities (g.6.c) is shown by completing research in spare time while working full-time. Professional qualifications (g.6.d) include MBChB, MFPH, and MPH.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details identified for this section.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details identified for this section.

sub_criteria.name: Quality of the Plain English Summary
sub_criteria.definition: Clear, simple description of the research that a non-specialist audience can understand; explains problem, importance, plan, beneficiaries, and real-world use; avoids jargon; aligns with the detailed proposal.

signals.signal_text: Readability scores (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid)
Low density of unexplained jargon or technical terms
Presence of problem statement, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact
Alignment with detailed proposal content
Sentence structure and coherence for non-specialist understanding
Emphasis on societal or patient relevance

sub_criteria.pros: The Plain English Summary is exceptionally clear, with a Flesch-Kincaid grade of 6.86 and very low jargon density (1.03%). It comprehensively covers the problem (discharge summaries unsuitable for care workers), objectives (4 specific questions), methods (survey, focus groups, AI), beneficiaries (care workers, residents), and impact (improved safety, reduced readmissions). The text aligns perfectly with the detailed research plan and uses simple sentence structures suitable for a non-specialist audience.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details found; the summary is a model of clarity and completeness.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details found; the summary is a model of clarity and completeness.

sub_criteria.name: Evidence Review Quality & Relevance
sub_criteria.definition: How comprehensively and critically the application reviews existing literature and evidence.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of references cited
Presence of critical analysis vs. mere summary
Identification of knowledge gaps
Coherence and logical flow

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides a robust review of existing evidence, citing specific studies on health literacy gaps in care workers and the poor readability of current discharge summaries (Flesch-Kincaid scores). It critically analyzes the mismatch between document complexity and carer skills, clearly identifying knowledge gaps regarding the specific health literacy levels of care workers and the lack of UK-based data on MDS readability for this audience.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the literature review is well-integrated and directly justifies the proposed study.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the literature review is well-integrated and directly justifies the proposed study.

sub_criteria.name: Research Scope & Award Appropriateness
sub_criteria.definition: Whether the project scope matches the scale and expectations of the proposed award (doctoral, postdoctoral, etc.).

signals.signal_text: Alignment of aims with award level
Feasibility indicators (time, complexity, tasks)
Proportion of work relative to award expectations

sub_criteria.pros: The project scope is highly appropriate for a doctoral fellowship, comprising four distinct work packages (systematic review, survey, mixed methods, AI feasibility) with clearly defined sample sizes (e.g., 156 surveys, 291 MDS) and timelines. The complexity is balanced by the applicant's prior experience and strong institutional support, ensuring feasibility within the 3-year award period.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the scope is ambitious yet realistic given the resources and support described.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the scope is ambitious yet realistic given the resources and support described.

sub_criteria.name: Research Design & Methodological Rigor
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity, appropriateness, and robustness of study design to answer the research questions.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of methods
Logical alignment between aims and methods
Discussion of controls, sample size, or methodology justification
Structured methodological sections

sub_criteria.pros: The research design is rigorous and explicitly detailed. Methods include validated tools (FHLT, PEMAT, eHEALS), PRISMA guidelines for the systematic review, and a modified Delphi method for consensus. Sample size calculations are provided with statistical justification. The alignment between aims and methods is logical, with clear plans for data analysis (R, NVivo) and quality control (mock examples reviewed by experts).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the methodological rigor is high and well-justified.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the methodological rigor is high and well-justified.

sub_criteria.name: Potential Impact on Patients, Public, and Health Services
sub_criteria.definition: Likely benefits or influence of the research on patients, carers, or health and care systems.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of direct or indirect patient/public benefits
Alignment with health priorities or societal needs
Clarity and specificity of expected outcomes

sub_criteria.pros: The application clearly articulates significant benefits for patients (improved safety, reduced readmissions), carers (reduced workload, clearer information), and the NHS (cost savings). The impact is specific, linking improved discharge summaries directly to better handover processes and patient outcomes. The plan addresses health inequalities by focusing on the underserved social care sector.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the impact pathway is clear and well-supported by the proposed outputs.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the impact pathway is clear and well-supported by the proposed outputs.

sub_criteria.name: Engagement with People & Communities
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful involvement of patients, carers, or communities.

signals.signal_text: Use of co-design, participatory methods, or consultations
References to ethical considerations and feedback mechanisms
Description of involvement at multiple project stages

sub_criteria.pros: Engagement is deeply integrated throughout the research lifecycle. The project question was co-produced with care workers, and the methodology was adapted based on feedback from a community group (e.g., printed materials for Delphi). A steering committee with carers, residents, and families will guide the project from protocol to dissemination. Ethical considerations and feedback mechanisms are explicitly planned.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the engagement plan is comprehensive and goes beyond tokenistic involvement.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the engagement plan is comprehensive and goes beyond tokenistic involvement.

sub_criteria.name: Justification of Resources & Award Costs
sub_criteria.definition: Appropriateness and clarity of requested resources for the planned work.

signals.signal_text: Explicit links between tasks and resources requested
Budget proportionality to project scope
Clear rationale for funding level

sub_criteria.pros: The budget is highly detailed with explicit justifications linking costs to specific work packages (e.g., statistician for WP1, NLP developer for WP4). Resources for participant compensation, travel, and accessibility equipment are clearly itemized. The total cost is proportional to the scope of a 3-year doctoral fellowship with significant data collection and AI development components.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the budget is well-justified and aligned with the project scope.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the budget is well-justified and aligned with the project scope.

sub_criteria.name: Quality of Previous Research (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: Prior relevant research experience demonstrating capability to deliver the proposed project.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of publications or outputs
Evidence of prior successful projects
Skills alignment with proposed work

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant demonstrates strong prior research capability with multiple peer-reviewed publications, conference presentations, and awards. They have successfully led a previous cross-sectional survey on health literacy, managed a team, and analyzed data in R. This experience directly aligns with the skills required for the proposed project (surveys, qualitative methods, data analysis).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the applicant's track record strongly supports their ability to deliver the proposed work.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the applicant's track record strongly supports their ability to deliver the proposed work.

sub_criteria.name: Inclusive Research Design
sub_criteria.definition: Consideration of inclusivity throughout the research lifecycle, including participant diversity and accessibility.

signals.signal_text: Explicit strategies for diverse recruitment
Adaptive methods or accessibility considerations
Equity and participation indicators

sub_criteria.pros: The design explicitly considers inclusivity through purposive recruitment strategies (diversity in health literacy, CQC ratings, region) and adaptive methods (printed materials, face-to-face options for those with digital barriers). The budget includes specific line items for accessibility equipment (large print keyboards, webcams) and compensation for participants, ensuring equity in participation.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the inclusive design is well-resourced and thoughtfully integrated.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the inclusive design is well-resourced and thoughtfully integrated.

sub_criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
sub_criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides a highly detailed, structured training plan explicitly mapped to each Work Package (WP1-WP4). It lists specific courses (e.g., UCL DSDP, SRA, Plain English Diploma), providers, and durations. The plan covers the full range of required methodologies: systematic reviews, survey design, statistical analysis (R), qualitative methods (NVivo, focus groups), and AI/NLP. The budget section (secsb__001_i) corroborates the training plan with specific line items for short courses, ensuring the plan is costed and actionable.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the plan is comprehensive, the sequencing (td.1.c) is described by WP association rather than a strict chronological timeline (e.g., Gantt chart for training specifically), though the overall project timeline is referenced. This is a minor gap in explicit temporal sequencing for the training itself, though the logical flow is clear.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the plan is comprehensive, the sequencing (td.1.c) is described by WP association rather than a strict chronological timeline (e.g., Gantt chart for training specifically), though the overall project timeline is referenced. This is a minor gap in explicit temporal sequencing for the training itself, though the logical flow is clear.

sub_criteria.name: Suitability to Personal Development and Project Needs
sub_criteria.definition: How well the proposed training addresses the applicant’s current skills gaps and supports successful project delivery.

signals.signal_text: Identification of personal skill gaps
Clear mapping between training activities and project tasks
Evidence that proposed training is targeted rather than generic
Mention of mentorship, workshops, or formal courses relevant to development needs

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant clearly identifies specific skill gaps (e.g., qualitative research, AI/NLP, facilitation) and maps them directly to targeted training activities. The plan is highly specific, citing exact course titles and providers (e.g., 'Digital qualitative interviews' by SRA, 'LlaMa' model courses). It explicitly builds on prior experience (e.g., 'refresh my knowledge' for systematic reviews, 'build on this' for qualitative methods) rather than offering generic training. Mentorship is integrated through specific supervisors (Prof Shallcross, Dr Shah) and external coaches (Dave Thornton, Emma Falkner) to support leadership and PPI facilitation skills.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details found. The training plan is perfectly aligned with the project needs and personal development gaps.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details found. The training plan is perfectly aligned with the project needs and personal development gaps.

sub_criteria.name: Support for Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: The extent to which the training plan, and any professional practice components, contribute to the applicant’s long-term career goals.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of career goals
Connections between training activities and future career trajectory
Indications of skill transferability beyond the current project
Reference to professional practice or experiential learning where applicable

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states career goals to become an independent researcher in public health and academia. The training plan directly supports this trajectory by including sessions on 'Research careers', 'Persuasive grant writing', 'Building your research pipeline', and 'The PhD Journey'. It emphasizes transferable skills such as facilitation, leadership, and dissemination (storytelling, infographic design) that extend beyond the immediate project. The inclusion of coaching for leadership development and PPI training further strengthens the link to long-term career aspirations in health and social care research.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details found. The connection between training and career goals is explicit and well-evidenced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details found. The connection between training and career goals is explicit and well-evidenced.

sub_criteria.name: Track Record of the Contracting Organisation (and Partners)
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrated expertise, experience, and research achievements of the organisation(s) in the proposed research area.

signals.signal_text: Publication or project history in relevant research fields
Previous funding success or awards in similar domains
Mention of prior collaborations or partnerships
Highlighted achievements or notable outputs

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides exceptional evidence of the contracting organisation's (UCL IHI) track record. It details specific high-impact projects (VIVALDI), extensive funding success (NIHR BRC, MRC), and a history of supervising PhDs to completion. The supervisors' profiles explicitly list publications, awards (MBE), and leadership roles in relevant fields (public health, social care, AI). The host organisation statement confirms a strong track record of supporting early career researchers with specific examples of career progression.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details found; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all aspects of the track record.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details found; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all aspects of the track record.

sub_criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
sub_criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

sub_criteria.pros: The application details a robust supervisory team with clearly defined roles and specific expertise matching the project's multidisciplinary needs (public health, qualitative methods, AI/NLP, health literacy). A structured supervision framework is explicitly described, including fortnightly meetings with the primary supervisor, monthly meetings with co-supervisors, and a thesis committee meeting 5 times. Mentorship plans include specific training support and risk register reviews.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the supervision plan is highly detailed and well-aligned with the project requirements.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the supervision plan is highly detailed and well-aligned with the project requirements.

sub_criteria.name: Appropriateness of the Contracting Organisation to Support the Award and Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: How well the organisation (and any partners) can provide the infrastructure, resources, and environment necessary to deliver the project and support the applicant’s career growth.

signals.signal_text: Description of facilities, infrastructure, or resources provided
Links between organisational support and project success
Alignment of organisational focus with applicant’s career trajectory
Evidence of tailored support for award type (doctoral, postdoctoral)

sub_criteria.pros: The application comprehensively describes the infrastructure and resources available, including the UCL-led Vivaldi network for recruitment, established links with three NHS trusts for data access, and access to AI/data science expertise at UCLH. The host organisation statement explicitly links these resources to project success and career growth, mentioning specific schemes for transitioning from PhD to post-doctoral positions (UCL Excellence Fellowships). The environment is described as supportive, with access to training, peer engagement, and a diverse leadership team.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the support infrastructure is clearly articulated and directly linked to the project's specific needs.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the support infrastructure is clearly articulated and directly linked to the project's specific needs.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Inclusive and Supportive Research Culture
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that the organisation fosters inclusivity, research integrity, and a supportive environment for all researchers.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of policies or practices promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion
Evidence of inclusive recruitment, mentoring, or support initiatives
References to adherence to research integrity principles
Statements on organisational culture, training, or initiatives supporting underrepresented groups

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides strong evidence of a commitment to an inclusive culture. It explicitly mentions UCL's Silver Athena Swan award and Race Equality Charter Bronze award. The host organisation statement details an active Equality, Diversity, and Inclusion (EDI) committee, an ethnically diverse senior leadership team, and specific initiatives to rotate leadership roles to widen opportunities. The statement also highlights a supportive environment for early career researchers and a commitment to an inclusive research culture.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the evidence of inclusive policies and culture is specific and well-supported by awards and structural descriptions.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the evidence of inclusive policies and culture is specific and well-supported by awards and structural descriptions.

sub_criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
sub_criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides exceptional evidence of lived experience integration. The research question was directly generated by a care worker during shadowing (secarb__001_e, secppi__001_a). Specific design changes were made based on community feedback, such as adapting the Delphi method to include printed materials and face-to-face options for older adults (secppi__001_b). A steering committee comprising carers, residents, and families is established to guide the project from protocol to dissemination (secsa__001_c, secdrp__001_s).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details found; the evidence is comprehensive and specific.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details found; the evidence is comprehensive and specific.

sub_criteria.name: Diversity and Inclusivity of the Research Team
sub_criteria.definition: Extent to which the research team reflects diversity in terms of skills, experience, and demographic representation.

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

sub_criteria.pros: The research team explicitly includes care workers, residents, and families on the steering committee, ensuring diverse representation beyond academic staff (secdrp__001_s, secppi__001_c). The host organization (UCL IHI) is noted to have an ethnically diverse senior leadership team (sectdars__001_s).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the PPI team is diverse, there is limited explicit detail regarding the demographic diversity (e.g., ethnicity, gender, disability) of the core academic research team or the specific strategies used to recruit a diverse academic team (wp.2.a). Statements about inclusion in hiring are not explicitly detailed for the research team itself (wp.2.b).
sub_criteria.rationale: While the PPI team is diverse, there is limited explicit detail regarding the demographic diversity (e.g., ethnicity, gender, disability) of the core academic research team or the specific strategies used to recruit a diverse academic team (wp.2.a). Statements about inclusion in hiring are not explicitly detailed for the research team itself (wp.2.b).

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

sub_criteria.pros: The application details a robust dissemination plan including scientific publications, grey literature for non-medical audiences, and a knowledge share event co-led with stakeholders (secdrp__001_u, secdrp__001_v). Clear links are drawn between study objectives (optimizing MDS) and anticipated benefits such as reduced workload, improved patient safety, and cost savings (secsa__001_c, secdrp__001_w). The applicant has a track record of translating findings into practice (e.g., improved appointment letters) (secarb__001_c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the plan is specific and actionable.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the plan is specific and actionable.

sub_criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
sub_criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly targets care home residents and care workers, groups often underrepresented in research (secppi__001_b). Specific barriers to participation (e.g., digital exclusion, difficulty with online meetings) are identified and mitigated through printed materials, face-to-face options, and technical accessories (secppi__001_b, secsb__001_d). Recruitment strategies include purposive selection to maximize diversity in health literacy and care home characteristics (secdrp__001_p).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While specific barriers for older adults are addressed, there is less explicit discussion of barriers related to other protected characteristics (e.g., language barriers for non-English speakers, though exclusion criteria mention English-only MDS) (wp.4.a).
sub_criteria.rationale: While specific barriers for older adults are addressed, there is less explicit discussion of barriers related to other protected characteristics (e.g., language barriers for non-English speakers, though exclusion criteria mention English-only MDS) (wp.4.a).

sub_criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

sub_criteria.pros: Inclusion is embedded across the lifecycle: design (co-production of question), conduct (accessible Delphi methods, plain English TORs), and dissemination (co-led events) (secppi__001_b, secppi__001_c). Accessibility is prioritized with technical accessories and pre-meetings to build confidence (secppi__001_c). The steering committee's terms of reference explicitly commit to using plain English and ensuring all voices are heard (secppi__001_c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While plans for ongoing evaluation are implied through the steering committee meetings, a specific documented plan for evaluating the inclusivity of the research process itself (e.g., feedback loops on the inclusivity of the methods) is less explicit than the inclusion strategies themselves (wp.5.c).
sub_criteria.rationale: While plans for ongoing evaluation are implied through the steering committee meetings, a specific documented plan for evaluating the inclusivity of the research process itself (e.g., feedback loops on the inclusivity of the methods) is less explicit than the inclusion strategies themselves (wp.5.c).

sub_criteria.name: Costed and Resourced Plan for Research Inclusion
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that research inclusion has been properly budgeted and resourced within the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Explicit budget line(s) for inclusion activities (PPI, outreach, recruitment support)
Mention of staff time, materials, or training dedicated to inclusion
Alignment between inclusion plan and allocated resources

sub_criteria.pros: The budget explicitly includes line items for PPIE involvement, participant vouchers (£15 for survey, £25 for focus groups), and technical accessories (webcams, large print keyboards) for steering committee members (secsb__001_f, secsb__001_g, secsb__001_h). Staff time is allocated for coaching care workers and facilitating inclusive meetings (secdrp__001_z, secppi__001_d). The resources align perfectly with the inclusion plan described in the PPI section.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the budgeting is detailed and directly supports the inclusion activities.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the budgeting is detailed and directly supports the inclusion activities.

sub_criteria.name: Use of Formatting, Headings, and Subheadings
sub_criteria.definition: Effective use of text formatting to make the application clear, navigable, and easy to read.

signals.signal_text: Presence and consistency of headings and subheadings
Use of bold or emphasis for key terms
Structured hierarchy of sections (logical nesting of headings)
Patterns detected via text parsing for formatting markers

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates excellent use of formatting with 10 distinct sections detected, 26 numbered heading-like lines, and 34 bullet/numbered list markers (secafa__001). The hierarchy is logical, moving from applicant context to research plan, PPI, training, and budget. The parser detected 17 emphasis markers, and the text shows clear use of bolding for key terms (e.g., 'Aim:', 'Objectives:', 'WP1') and structured tables for the budget and CV.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While emphasis markers are present, the parser notes that bold/emphasis may be lost during text extraction, so the score for af.1.b is slightly reduced to 4 to account for the inability to verify the visual weight of all emphasis in the raw text stream, though the structural markers are robust.
sub_criteria.rationale: While emphasis markers are present, the parser notes that bold/emphasis may be lost during text extraction, so the score for af.1.b is slightly reduced to 4 to account for the inability to verify the visual weight of all emphasis in the raw text stream, though the structural markers are robust.

sub_criteria.name: Limited Duplication and Repetition
sub_criteria.definition: Avoidance of unnecessary repetition within or across sections.

signals.signal_text: Semantic similarity measures between sentences/paragraphs
Detection of repeated phrases or ideas
Novelty score for each section (unique content vs. overlap)

sub_criteria.pros: The application exhibits very low duplication rates. The duplicate sentence rate excluding budget is only 0.003, and the repeated line rate is 0.067 (secafa__001). While there is some expected lexical overlap between related sections (e.g., Plain English Summary and Scientific Abstract at 0.242), this represents necessary alignment rather than unnecessary repetition. Each section maintains high novelty relative to others.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the overlap metrics are within acceptable ranges for a comprehensive grant application where the problem statement and methodology must be reiterated in different levels of detail.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the overlap metrics are within acceptable ranges for a comprehensive grant application where the problem statement and methodology must be reiterated in different levels of detail.

sub_criteria.name: Logical Flow and Coherence Across Sections
sub_criteria.definition: The application progresses logically, with a clear thread linking the research question, objectives, and work packages.

signals.signal_text: Semantic coherence between sections (using embeddings or similarity metrics)
Explicit linking of objectives to methods and work packages
Presence of transitional phrases or linking statements
Detectable narrative thread anchored on research aims

sub_criteria.pros: The application shows strong logical flow with 32 transition phrases and 95 objective-method-budget link terms detected (secafa__001). The section order follows a standard, logical progression: Applicant -> Plain English -> Abstract -> Detailed Plan -> PPI -> Training -> Budget. The narrative thread is anchored clearly on the research aims, with explicit links between the problem statement, work packages, and budget justifications.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the structural analysis confirms a coherent and well-linked narrative throughout the document.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the structural analysis confirms a coherent and well-linked narrative throughout the document.
